raw_case:
Tuberous Sclerosis Complex Presenting as Periungual Fibromas: A Rare Case Report and Literature Review


Background

Tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC) is a rare autosomal-dominant disorder involving multiple organs including skin, brain, heart, lung, kidney and liver. It usually occurs as early as birth or even in utero, with rare cases diagnosed in their adulthood. Here, we present a rare adult case of TSC presenting as periungual fibromas (PF).

Case Presentation

A 67-year-old gentleman showed recurrence of multiple periungual polypoid tumors on all the toes of the right foot when presenting to our department. On physical examination, there were polypoid and verrucous protrusions on the nail fold side of the proximal toe. Computed tomography scan indicated multiple subependymal nodules and renal cyst. Pathological analysis for the polypoid tissues showed fibroepithelial-like lesions, epidermal hyperkeratosis, and acanthosis. Therefore, the patient was diagnosed with TSC presenting as PF.

Conclusion

We reported a rare case of TSC diagnosed in the adulthood based on the presence of PF, subependymal nodules, and renal cyst.

Introduction

Tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC) refers to a rare autosomal-dominant disorder that can affect any sex and ethnic group.1 Generally, it is a rare condition with multi-organ involvement, including skin, brain, heart, lung, kidney and liver. As a genetic disease, the individuals born with TSC show signs as early as birth or even in utero,2,3 with extremely rare cases being diagnosed in adulthood.There are no definite clinical manifestations in the musculoskeletal tissues for the TSC patients. The dermatological manifestations are usually typical, including café au lait spots (CALS), angiofibroma, periungual fibroma, intraoral fibroma, and enamel pits.4 The diagnostic criteria for TSC have been updated in 2021 by the International TSC Consensus Group, which mainly relies on clinical features and gene mutation analysis.5 According to these criteria, pathogenic mutation of TSC1 or TSC2 gene constitutes a definite diagnosis. Besides, the presence of two major features or one major feature with at least two minor features could also make a definite diagnosis.6 To date, the systemic treatment of TSC is mainly based on the mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) inhibitor; however, the optimal dose, appropriate serum concentration, treatment duration and long-term adverse events are still needed to be further explored. In this study, we reported a rare adult case of TSC presenting as periungual fibromas (PF). In addition, we performed a comprehensive literature research, with an aim to screen the TSC patients presenting with PF, followed by analyzing their clinical and genetic features.

Case Presentation

A 67-year-old gentleman presented to our department due to recurrence of multiple periungual polypoid tumors on all the toes of the right foot. He reported a polypoid tumor history for at least 30 years. Seven year ago, he showed seizure once, and then no recurrence was reported afterwards. On physical examination, there were polypoid and verrucous protrusions on the nail fold side of the proximal toe (Figure 1). He showed no clinical manifestations in the musculoskeletal tissues. The concentrations of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), neuron-specific enolase (NSE), Carbohydrate antigen 19–9 (CA19-9), CA724 and prostate-specific antigen (PSA) were in their normal ranges. Computed tomography (CT) scan indicated multiple subependymal nodules (Figure 2A and B), together with renal cyst (Figure 2C). Pathological analysis indicated fibroepithelial-like lesions, epidermal hyperkeratosis, and acanthosis. Besides, this was accompanied by thickened branched spinous processes, fibroblasts and dense collagen tissue in the center, and multiple dilated vascular components (Figure 3). Therefore, the patient was diagnosed with TSC presenting as PF. For the treatment, the patient merely received a local resection to remove the tumor in toes. In the recent follow-up, the patient showed no recurrence. The study protocol was consistent with the ethical principles of the Helsinki and was approved by the Ethics Committee of the Sanya Central Hospital (The Third People’s Hospital of Hainan Province). Written informed consent was obtained from the patient.


Discussion and Conclusions

TSC is a relatively rare autosomal dominant genetic disorder with an incidence of 1:6000–1:10,000.7 Patients with TSC could show hamartomas in any organ. The classic triad of TSC includes facial angiofibromas, mental retardation, and intractable epilepsy, which usually presents in childhood.8 In fact, merely 30% of patients show all the three characteristics, and multisystem manifestations vary substantially, which makes it a challenge for the diagnosis.9 TSC patients show increased risks of developing multiple benign or malignant tumors in various organs. As one of the tumors that cause nail deformities of the digits adjacent to the proximal or lateral nail folds, PF in TSC patients are presenting as multiple slowly growing, elongated or oval, flesh-colored lesions with distal hyperkeratosis.10 These patients usually show distortion of the nail plate, involving both fingers and toes. In this case report, we reported a TSC patient with PF involving the toes, and summarized the clinical and genetic features based on a literature review.In 2021, the International Tuberous Sclerosis Consensus Conference proposed diagnostic criteria for tuberous sclerosis, including clinical and genetic diagnostic criteria.5 The clinical diagnostic standards were divided into primary and secondary standards. The major criteria were as follows: hypomelanotic macules, angiofibroma or fibrous cephalic plaque, ungual fibromas, shagreen patch, multiple retinal hamartomas, multiple cortical tubers and/or radial migration lines, subependymal nodule, subependymal giant cell astrocytoma, cardiac rhabdomyoma, lymphangiomyomatosis, and angiomyolipomas (AML). The minor criteria were as follows: “Confetti” skin lesions, dental enamel pits, intraoral fibromas, retinal achromic patch, multiple renal cysts, nonrenal hamartomas, and sclerotic bone lesions.5 The genetic criteria for the TSC diagnosis was based on the pathogenic mutations of TSC1 or TSC2. However, approximately 10–25% of TSC patients showed no pathogenic mutations in TSC1 or TSC2. TSC1 and TSC2 variants whose effect on protein synthesis or function were not definitely pathogenic, and would not be considered diagnostic unless supported by additional ACMG criteria for pathogenicity.11 In this study, the patient did not receive genetic test due to financial causes; however, this would not affect the final diagnosis of TSC as the patient showed presence of PF (≥2) and subependymal nodule.TSC patients often show central nervous system (CNS) involvement, with epileptic seizures as the most commonly reported symptoms.12 About 63% of TSC patients experienced at least one epileptic seizure.13,14 For those with CNS involvement, the subependymal nodules, cortical dysplasia, and subependymal giant cell astrocytomas are the most common symptoms.6 In this case, the patient merely showed an epileptic seizure 7 years ago, with no recurrence afterwards. CT examination revealed that the patient had multiple subependymal nodules. This implied that imaging was critical for the diagnosis of TSC.Renal manifestations are the second most common findings associated with TSC, with AML occurring in 80% and renal cystic disease in 50% of patients.12 Most of the patients with AML are asymptomatic. However, the AML progression may lead to hematuria and renal failure, which is considered as the main cause of TSC-related death.15 An AML diameter of 30 mm or more would lead to increased risk of renal or hepatic hemorrhage and rupture.16–18 The treatment of TSC-related renal angioleiomyoma is mainly based on the principle of preserving renal function and prolonging the quality of life mainly including selective renal artery embolization, surgical resection, and radiofrequency ablation. Our case showed cystic lesions in the right kidney, and no additional management was required during the hospitalization. In the future, more attention should be paid to the renal function.TSC is also accompanied by various skin symptoms, including skin depigmentation spots, angiofibromas, connective tissue nevus, PF, as well as gingival tumors. The tumors originated from the nail fold area on the proximal side of the foot, which were nodular and verrucous in appearance. The development of the nail plate was disrupted and partially resulted in the absence of the nail plate. Ma et al19 reported that the histological changes of PF were mainly composed of different proportions of fibroblasts, collagen fibers and blood vessels. The epidermal changes were characterized by keratinization, hyperacanthosis, granular cell thickening and other changes. In this study, we reported a rare case of TSC presenting as PF, with multiple PF on both feet. Actually, it is still a challenge to define the proportion of subungual fibroma who would be eventually confirmed with TSC, as individuals with subungual fibroma were recommended but not necessary to undergo TSC screening. To further illustrate the clinical and genetic features of TSC, we performed a comprehensive literature research to the published articles in PubMed until January 2024. Case reports were collected by conducting a PubMed search with the term “tuberous sclerosis complex”, “periungual fibromas” or “ungual fibromas”, and “case reports”. Eventually, we found a total of 11 TSC patients presenting PF, including 10 published case reports9,20–28 and our case (Table 1). According to the literature review, the TSC could involve almost all organs, with no racial or gender preferences. Although great advances have been made in the management of TSC, its prognosis remains poor with the median patient age at death of approximately 33 years.6 In this case report, our patient was aged 67 years upon diagnosis, and the seizure only onset once. He was diagnosed based on the presence of major features of TSC (ie ungual fibromas and subependymal nodules) and minor features (ie renal cyst).
In summary, we reported a rare case report of TSC presenting PF at the age of 67. The patient initially presented with multiple PF on the toes, and then TSC was highly suspected. Then, CT scan was performed, which showed subependymal nodules and renal cyst. Finally, the patient was diagnosed with TSC, and he merely received surgery for the treatment. No recurrence was reported in the follow-up.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information**: 67-year-old male.
- **Chief Complaint**: Recurrence of multiple periungual polypoid tumors on all toes of the right foot.
- **History of Present Illness**: At least 30 years of recurring polypoid tumors on the toes. A single seizure occurred seven years ago but did not recur.
- **Past Medical History**: None reported.
- **Personal History**: No significant details provided.
- **Family History**: None reported.
- **Physical Examination**: Polypoid and verrucous protrusions on the nail fold side of the proximal toes. No musculoskeletal manifestations observed.
- **Ancillary Tests**:
  - Lab Results: Normal levels of tumor markers, such as CEA, AFP, NSE, CA19-9, CA724, PSA.
  - Imaging: CT scan revealed multiple subependymal nodules and a renal cyst.
  - Pathology: Fibroepithelial-like lesions, epidermal hyperkeratosis, acanthosis, thickened branched spinous processes, fibroblasts, dense collagen, and dilated vascular components.

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Considerations**:
   - Periungual fibromas as a primary clinical manifestation raised suspicion of a genetic or systemic condition.
   - Significant systemic findings, including subependymal nodules and renal cysts, aligned with potential diagnoses involving multisystem manifestations.

2. **Key Differentials**:
   - **Tuberous Sclerosis Complex (TSC)**:
     - *Rationale for Consideration*: TSC manifests with periungual fibromas, subependymal nodules, and renal cysts, all of which were present in this patient. The diagnostic criteria for TSC include either two major features or one major feature plus at least two minor features. The patient met these criteria based on the presence of ungual fibromas (major), subependymal nodules (major), and renal cyst (minor).
     - *Conclusion*: Strongly favored based on clinical and imaging findings.
   - **Non-TSC-Related Periungual Fibromas**:
     - *Rationale for Exclusion*: The concomitant findings of subependymal nodules and a renal cyst make isolated fibromas less likely.
   - **Neurofibromatosis**:
     - *Rationale for Exclusion*: Absence of café-au-lait macules, Lisch nodules, neurofibromas, or a family history of neurofibromatosis.
   - **Malignancy or Paraneoplastic Syndrome**:
     - *Rationale for Exclusion*: Lack of abnormal tumor markers or systemic malignancy on imaging.
   
3. **Diagnostic Confirmation**:
   - The diagnosis of TSC was strongly supported based on:
     - Major criteria: multiple ungual fibromas, subependymal nodules.
     - Minor criteria: multiple renal cysts.
     - No genetic testing was performed due to financial constraints, but clinical criteria were sufficient.

---
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
The final diagnosis is **Tuberous Sclerosis Complex (TSC)**, presenting with periungual fibromas, subependymal nodules, and renal cysts. The diagnosis was based on clinical and imaging findings meeting the 2021 International TSC Consensus Group criteria.

---

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's see. The patient is a 67-year-old male with recurring periungual polypoid tumors on all toes of the right foot for at least 30 years. He had a single seizure seven years ago. No significant past or family medical history. Physical exam shows these polypoid and verrucous growths on the nail folds. Lab tests are normal for tumor markers. CT scan shows subependymal nodules and a renal cyst. Pathology shows fibroepithelial-like lesions with hyperkeratosis, acanthosis, thickened spinous processes, fibroblasts, dense collagen, and dilated vessels.

First, I need to consider what causes periungual tumors. Common things could be warts, fibromas, maybe even squamous cell carcinoma, but the long history and recurrence suggest something chronic. Since the tumor markers are normal, malignancy is less likely.

The seizure history is interesting. Subependymal nodules on CT make me think of tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC). TSC is a genetic disorder that causes benign tumors in multiple organs, including the brain (subependymal nodules), kidneys (cysts or angiomyolipomas), skin, etc. Skin manifestations include angiofibromas, periungual fibromas (Koenen tumors), shagreen patches. The periungual growths here could be Koenen tumors, which are a major diagnostic criterion for TSC.

Pathology findings of fibroepithelial lesions with vascular components align with periungual fibromas. The subependymal nodules and renal cyst also fit with TSC. The seizure could be due to cortical tubers or other brain lesions associated with TSC. Even though he only had one seizure, TSC can have variable neurological presentations.

Other possibilities: multiple warts (HPV-related), but the location and pathology don't fit. Fibroepithelial polyps are possible, but the association with subependymal nodules and renal cysts points more towards a systemic condition like TSC. The renal cyst isn't a classic angiomyolipoma, but TSC can have various renal manifestations including cysts.

Family history is negative, but TSC can occur from spontaneous mutations. The combination of skin lesions, neurological findings (seizure and subependymal nodules), and renal involvement makes TSC the most likely diagnosis.
results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient’s long-standing periungual polypoid tumors, recurring over 30 years, suggest a chronic benign condition rather than malignancy, especially given normal tumor markers.  
<step 2> The CT findings of subependymal nodules and renal cyst raise suspicion for tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC), a multisystem disorder associated with hamartomas in multiple organs.  
<step 3> Periungual fibromas (Koenen tumors) are a hallmark of TSC and align with the described polypoid lesions. The pathology (fibroepithelial lesions, vascular proliferation) supports this diagnosis.  
<step 4> The patient’s history of a seizure correlates with TSC-related neurological manifestations, such as cortical tubers or subependymal nodules. Renal cysts are also consistent with TSC spectrum.  
<step 5> Other differentials (e.g., viral warts, fibroepithelial polyps) lack systemic features seen here, solidifying TSC as the unifying diagnosis despite negative family history (possible de novo mutation).  

### Answer:
Tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC) with periungual fibromas (Koenen tumors)

reasoning_eval.step: The patient’s long-standing periungual polypoid tumors, recurring over 30 years, suggest a chronic benign condition rather than malignancy, especially given normal tumor markers.
The CT findings of subependymal nodules and renal cyst raise suspicion for tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC), a multisystem disorder associated with hamartomas in multiple organs.
Periungual fibromas (Koenen tumors) are a hallmark of TSC and align with the described polypoid lesions. The pathology (fibroepithelial lesions, vascular proliferation) supports this diagnosis.
The patient’s history of a seizure correlates with TSC-related neurological manifestations, such as cortical tubers or subependymal nodules. Renal cysts are also consistent with TSC spectrum.

Other differentials (e.g., viral warts, fibroepithelial polyps) lack systemic features seen here, solidifying TSC as the unifying diagnosis despite negative family history (possible de novo mutation).

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: Periungual fibromas, subependymal nodules, and renal cysts raised suspicion of a genetic or systemic condition.

Step 2: Tuberous Sclerosis Complex (TSC) was strongly favored based on the presence of periungual fibromas (major criterion), subependymal nodules (major criterion), and renal cysts (minor criterion).
Step 3: Non-TSC-related periungual fibromas were excluded due to the presence of subependymal nodules and renal cysts.
Step 4: Neurofibromatosis was excluded due to the absence of café-au-lait macules, Lisch nodules, neurofibromas, and a family history.
Step 5: Malignancy or paraneoplastic syndrome was excluded due to the lack of abnormal tumor markers or systemic malignancy on imaging.
Step 6: The diagnosis of TSC was confirmed based on meeting the 2021 International TSC Consensus Group criteria with major and minor criteria.
Step 7: Genetic testing was not performed due to financial constraints, but clinical and imaging findings were sufficient for diagnosis.
Step 8: The final diagnosis is Tuberous Sclerosis Complex (TSC), presenting with periungual fibromas, subependymal nodules, and renal cysts.
